title: Towards Continuous User Recognition by Exploring Physiological Multimodality: An Electrocardiogram (ECG) and Blood Volume Pulse (BVP) Approach

method.training_strategy: weighted 3-NN classifier with Bayesian decision fusion

evaluation.context: Best achieved for Forearm ECG modality during within-session across-task (WS-AT) analysis using task S1.3

evaluation_protocol: Leave-one-out (LOO) with within-session within-task (WS-WT), within-session across-task (WS-AT), and across-session (AS) analysis.

reported_limitations: BVP modality degrades performance due to significant noise
Finger movements severely contaminate the BVP signal with motion artifacts
Forearm ECG valid data percentage depends heavily on user interaction behavior

usability.usability_issues: BVP signal is highly sensitive to finger movements during HCI tasks.

key_contribution: Evaluation of ECG and BVP for continuous recognition in an HCI setup
Analysis of the impact of ECG window length (PQRST vs PQRS vs QRS) on temporal stability
Investigation of within-session vs across-session performance degradation
main_strength: Use of task-based simulations to assess usability in a workspace environment
main_weakness: BVP modality provided little to no benefit and was highly noise-prone
Small sample size for across-session tests (15 subjects)
insights.notes_for_review: The paper concludes that the PQRS complex of the ECG is more robust to temporal changes than the full PQRST complex.
